hook: $40 killed the 'institution' look in my sister's dorm

scenes.visual_prompt: Bare dorm window with harsh afternoon glare hitting a cinderblock wall, fluorescent overhead light on
scenes.voiceover: Bare windows and fluorescent overhead lighting — nothing says temporary institution louder.

scenes.visual_prompt: Hands installing a tension rod over a dorm window, no drilling, then hanging blackout curtain panels
scenes.voiceover: I hung blackout curtain panels on a tension rod, since most dorms don't allow drilled hardware.

scenes.visual_prompt: Before and after shot of the same window, harsh glare vs blocked light with curtain closed
scenes.voiceover: They cut the harsh afternoon glare that had the room feeling like a waiting room.

scenes.visual_prompt: Close-up of stiff blackout curtain fabric compared to airy sheer curtains in a reference photo
scenes.on_screen_text: Honest flaw: stiffer than Pinterest photos
scenes.voiceover: They look slightly stiffer than the airy sheers you see in dorm-decor photos, but they actually block sound and light.

scenes.visual_prompt: Warm LED string lights being clipped along a window frame with adhesive clips
scenes.voiceover: I paired them with warm LED string lights along the window frame — the cheapest change that made the room photograph well.

scenes.visual_prompt: Close-up of adhesive clip label checking wall-damage compatibility against a dorm handbook page
scenes.voiceover: If your dorm restricts wall damage, check that your light clips are rated for painted cinderblock before you buy.

scenes.visual_prompt: Wide finished shot of the window at dusk, curtains closed halfway and string lights glowing
scenes.voiceover: Fifteen minutes, no tools beyond a tension rod, and the room stopped looking temporary.

caption: $40 and 15 minutes is all it took to stop this room from looking like a waiting room. Details + the handbook check you need to make first, on the blog.
